skill.name: json-canvas
skill.description: Create and edit Obsidian JSON Canvas files with text, file, link, group, and edge structures.
skill.summary: Build valid .canvas diagrams for Obsidian using the JSON Canvas specification.
skill.author: kepano

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, C2, and weak-crypto indicators, but review found markdown code fences, JSON field names, and example hex IDs rather than executable behavior. No prompt injection, malicious intent, network calls, or hidden scripts were found. The remaining concerns are normal low-risk capabilities: editing local .canvas files and allowing link nodes with external URLs.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Dismissed
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby and shell backtick matches are markdown code fences and inline JSON examples. I found no executable script, shell command, or command invocation behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited regions are fenced JSON examples, not Ruby or shell execution. The skill repository contains documentation files only.

low_findings.title: Static C2 And Weak Crypto Alerts Dismissed
low_findings.description: The high-severity alerts match JSON Canvas identifiers, fromNode and toNode fields, arrow endpoints, and sample hexadecimal IDs. These are data model examples, not command-and-control or cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic review shows these tokens describe graph edges and unique canvas IDs. No encryption routine, beacon logic, or remote-control workflow is present.

low_findings.title: Local File Edits And External Links Need Normal Scope Control
low_findings.description: The skill intentionally reads and writes .canvas files and supports link nodes with URLs. This is expected behavior, but users should review file paths and destination URLs before applying changes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-edit and URL capabilities are clearly documented and legitimate for this skill. The risk is limited to user-controlled file scope and link review.

content.value_statement: Building .canvas files by hand is error-prone. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create, edit, and validate JSON Canvas layouts.
actual_capabilities: Creates new .canvas files with nodes and edges.
Adds text, file, link, and group nodes using JSON Canvas fields.
Connects nodes with labeled edges and optional arrow endpoints.
Edits existing canvases while preserving valid node references.
Checks IDs, edge references, color values, and JSON parseability.
Provides complete examples for mind maps, boards, research canvases, and flowcharts.
limitations: Does not render a visual preview of the canvas.
Does not verify whether referenced local files exist.
Does not fetch or validate external link destinations.
Depends on the user or host tool to write files safely.

use_cases.title: Plan Visual Notes
use_cases.description: Create structured Obsidian canvases for ideas, outlines, and connected notes.

use_cases.title: Map Project Workflows
use_cases.description: Build project boards, process diagrams, and flowcharts with groups and labeled edges.

use_cases.title: Organize Research Sources
use_cases.description: Connect notes, files, images, and reference links into a single research canvas.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Basic Canvas
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new visual note.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a JSON Canvas for these five ideas. Use one central topic, four supporting text nodes, and clear connections.

prompt_templates.title: Add Nodes To My Canvas
prompt_templates.prompt: Read my existing .canvas file and add three new text nodes. Place them without overlap and connect them to the relevant nodes.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Project Board
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Obsidian Canvas project board with To Do, In Progress, Review, and Done groups. Add tasks and status connections.

prompt_templates.title: Validate And Repair A Canvas
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced cleanup of a broken canvas.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this .canvas file for duplicate IDs, missing node references, invalid colors, and malformed text. Repair issues and explain changes.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a mind map about a product launch plan.
output_examples.output: A valid .canvas file with a central launch node, grouped planning areas, connected task nodes, and readable spacing.

output_examples.input: A user provides an existing canvas with broken connections.
output_examples.output: A corrected canvas where all edge references resolve, duplicate identifiers are replaced, and layout spacing is preserved.

output_examples.input: A user wants a research canvas for papers, notes, and links.
output_examples.output: A research canvas that links source files, meeting notes, external references, and synthesis nodes with labeled relationships.

best_practices: Keep node IDs unique across nodes and edges.
Leave enough spacing so nodes do not overlap in Obsidian.
Validate JSON and edge references after every edit.
anti_patterns: Do not reuse node IDs across unrelated canvas items.
Do not create edges before confirming both endpoint nodes exist.
Do not insert unescaped line breaks inside text values.

faq.question: What is JSON Canvas?
faq.answer: JSON Canvas is a file format for visual canvases with nodes, groups, links, and connecting edges.

faq.question: Does this skill work with Obsidian Canvas?
faq.answer: Yes. It follows the JSON Canvas structure used by Obsidian .canvas files.

faq.question: Can it edit an existing canvas?
faq.answer: Yes. It can parse an existing .canvas file, update nodes or edges, and revalidate references.

faq.question: Can it preview the canvas visually?
faq.answer: No. It creates and edits the underlying file, but it does not render an interactive preview.

faq.question: Can it add file and link nodes?
faq.answer: Yes. It can add file nodes with vault paths and link nodes with external URLs.

faq.question: What should I check before saving changes?
faq.answer: Review file paths, external URLs, node positions, unique IDs, and edge endpoints before writing the file.
